rag_file.text_content:
{"apiVersion":"2024-11-30","modelId":"prebuilt-layout","stringIndexType":"textElements","content":"Title: Developing AI tailored immunocytokines to target melanoma brain metastases

Background: Brain metastases (BMs) occur in almost 50% of patients with metastatic melanoma, resulting in a dismal prognosis with a poor overall survival for most patients. Metastases arise from primary tumor clones capable of adapting to new environments and are frequently resistant to therapies to which the primary tumor was sensitive. Particularly in BMs, the milieu of the brain creates a highly immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). Single-cell technologies together with machine learning have emerged as powerful tools to decipher complex interactions between cells in the TME, enabling development of data driven designs of immunotherapies. In this project, we aim to harness these tools to gain in depth understanding of melanoma BM TMEs immunosuppression, and to develop novel cytokine-based approaches that turn the BM TME from immunologically “cold” to “hot”.

Aim 1: Developing BM TME models with holistic, multimodal AI-driven analysis. We will rely on orthotopic immunocompetent murine models for melanoma BMs developed by the Weiss group together with the large cohort of patient clinical samples collected by the Weiss group and our collaborators in hospitals in Israel. These will be used to create single-cell atlases of immune, stromal and tumor cells from human and murine BMs. We will go significantly beyond the state of the art, by building integrated tissue models from single cells and holistically inferring their physiology and potential drug response. We will use novel technology developed in our groups for cell temporal tracking (ZMAN-seq, differentiation flows), cell-cell interactions (PIC-seq), and improved strategies for AI analysis of spatial transcriptomics with Stereo-seq. Our tools will identify shared processes and pathways between our models and human patient TMEs, enabling us to compile a list of cytokines with potentially high antitumor activity in melanoma BMs.

Aim 2: Preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models. The cytokines identified in Aim 1 will be tested in vitro and in vivo. First, cytokine-secreting clones of brain tropic melanoma cell lines will be created by the Amit group by transducing them to conditionally (dox-induced) express a broad spectrum of promising BM immune re-activation candidate cytokines. These clones will be used to create tumor spheroids containing one or multiple cytokine cassettes and will be co-cultured with immune cells. By adding doxycycline, we will test varied cytokine combinations. The most effective cytokines and cytokine combinations identified in vitro will be used by the Weiss group for in vivo testing. The B16 clones expressing the shortlisted cytokines or combinations will be used to generate melanoma BMs by intracardial injections. Cytokine expression will be activated with doxycycline and the tumor response will be monitored using MRI and survival measurement.

Aim 3: Design of tumor-targeting immunocytokines. Potent cytokines that reactivate the immune compartment and eradicated melanoma BMs will be incorporated into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins (immunocytokines) targeting species-conserved tumor antigens highly expressed in melanoma BMs. Employing melanoma BMs murine models and our advanced technologies from previous objectives, we aim at breaking through the classical dichotomy of response/no-response and into cycles of model-driven optimization. This will ultimately pinpoint the most effective constructs for the IND stage.

Impact: Our team of world leaders in immunology, brain tumors and computer science research is ideally suited to deliver this disruptive proposal. Ido Amit, an authority in single-cell immuno-oncology, and Amos Tanay, a leading figure in algorithm development for single cell biology, have co-developed several innovative single-cell computational tools. Tobias Weiss, an early-stage physician-scientist specializing in brain malignancies, developed promising immunocytokines against glioblastoma, now in clinical trials. The team has recently published and demonstrated that immunocytokines induce significant anti-tumor responses in both mice and glioblastoma patients.

Developing AI tailored immunocytokines to target melanoma brain metastases"},{"spans":[{"offset":83,"length":882}],"content":"Background: Brain metastases (BMs) occur in almost 50% of patients with metastatic melanoma, resulting in a dismal prognosis with a poor overall survival for most patients. Metastases arise from primary tumor clones capable of adapting to new environments and are frequently resistant to therapies to which the primary tumor was sensitive. Particularly in BMs, the milieu of the brain creates a highly immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). Single-cell technologies together with machine learning have emerged as powerful tools to decipher complex interactions between cells in the TME, enabling development of data driven designs of immunotherapies. In this project, we aim to harness these tools to gain in depth understanding of melanoma BM TMEs immunosuppression, and to develop novel cytokine-based approaches that turn the BM TME from immunologically “cold” to “hot”."},{"spans":[{"offset":967,"length":1038}],"content":"Aim 1: Developing BM TME models with holistic, multimodal AI-driven analysis. We will rely on orthotopic immunocompetent murine models for melanoma BMs developed by the Weiss group together with the large cohort of patient clinical samples collected by the Weiss group and our collaborators in hospitals in Israel. These will be used to create single-cell atlases of immune, stromal and tumor cells from human and murine BMs. We will go significantly beyond the state of the art, by building integrated tissue models from single cells and holistically inferring their physiology and potential drug response. We will use novel technology developed in our groups for cell temporal tracking (ZMAN-seq, differentiation flows), cell-cell interactions (PIC-seq), and improved strategies for AI analysis of spatial transcriptomics with Stereo-seq. Our tools will identify shared processes and pathways between our models and human patient TMEs, enabling us to compile a list of cytokines with potentially high antitumor activity in melanoma BMs."},{"spans":[{"offset":2007,"length":991}],"content":"Aim 2: Preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models. The cytokines identified in Aim 1 will be tested in vitro and in vivo. First, cytokine-secreting clones of brain tropic melanoma cell lines will be created by the Amit group by transducing them to conditionally (dox-induced) express a broad spectrum of promising BM immune re-activation candidate cytokines. These clones will be used to create tumor spheroids containing one or multiple cytokine cassettes and will be co-cultured with immune cells. By adding doxycycline, we will test varied cytokine combinations. The most effective cytokines and cytokine combinations identified in vitro will be used by the Weiss group for in vivo testing. The B16 clones expressing the shortlisted cytokines or combinations will be used to generate melanoma BMs by intracardial injections. Cytokine expression will be activated with doxycycline and the tumor response will be monitored using MRI and survival measurement."},{"spans":[{"offset":3000,"length":581}],"content":"Aim 3: Design of tumor-targeting immunocytokines. Potent cytokines that reactivate the immune compartment and eradicated melanoma BMs will be incorporated into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins (immunocytokines) targeting species-conserved tumor antigens highly expressed in melanoma BMs. Employing melanoma BMs murine models and our advanced technologies from previous objectives, we aim at breaking through the classical dichotomy of response/no-response and into cycles of model-driven optimization. This will ultimately pinpoint the most effective constructs for the IND stage."},{"spans":[{"offset":3583,"length":671}],"content":"Impact: Our team of world leaders in immunology, brain tumors and computer science research is ideally suited to deliver this disruptive proposal. Ido Amit, an authority in single-cell immuno-oncology, and Amos Tanay, a leading figure in algorithm development for single cell biology, have co-developed several innovative single-cell computational tools. Tobias Weiss, an early-stage physician-scientist specializing in brain malignancies, developed promising immunocytokines against glioblastoma, now in clinical trials. The team has recently published and demonstrated that immunocytokines induce significant anti-tumor responses in both mice and glioblastoma patients."}],"contentFormat":"markdown","sections":[{"spans":[{"offset":0,"length":4254}],"elements":["/paragraphs/0","/paragraphs/1","/paragraphs/2","/paragraphs/3","/paragraphs/4","/paragraphs/5"]}]}

chunk.content: Title: Developing AI tailored immunocytokines to target melanoma brain metastases Background: Brain metastases (BMs) occur in almost 50% of patients with metastatic melanoma, resulting in a dismal prognosis with a poor overall survival for most patients. Metastases arise from primary tumor clones capable of adapting to new environments and are frequently resistant to therapies to which the primary tumor was sensitive. Particularly in BMs, the milieu of the brain creates a highly immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). Single-cell technologies together with machine learning have emerged as powerful tools to decipher complex interactions between cells in the TME, enabling development of data driven designs of immunotherapies. In this project, we aim to harness these tools to gain in depth understanding of melanoma BM TMEs immunosuppression, and to develop novel cytokine-based approaches that turn the BM TME from immunologically “cold” to “hot”. Aim 1: Developing BM TME models with holistic, multimodal AI-driven analysis. We will rely on orthotopic immunocompetent murine models for melanoma BMs developed by the Weiss group together with the large cohort of patient clinical samples collected by the Weiss group and our collaborators in hospitals in Israel. These will be used to create single-cell atlases of immune, stromal and tumor cells from human and murine BMs. We will go significantly beyond the state of the art, by building integrated tissue models from single cells and holistically inferring their physiology and potential drug response. We will use novel technology developed in our groups for cell temporal tracking (ZMAN-seq, differentiation flows), cell-cell interactions (PIC-seq), and improved strategies for AI analysis of spatial transcriptomics with Stereo-seq.

Our tools will identify shared processes and pathways between our models and human patient TMEs, enabling us to compile a list of cytokines with potentially high antitumor activity in melanoma BMs. Aim 2: Preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models. The cytokines identified in Aim 1 will be tested in vitro and in vivo. First, cytokine-secreting clones of brain tropic melanoma cell lines will be created by the Amit group by transducing them to conditionally (dox-induced) express a broad spectrum of promising BM immune re-activation candidate cytokines. These clones will be used to create tumor spheroids containing one or multiple cytokine cassettes and will be co-cultured with immune cells. By adding doxycycline, we will test varied cytokine combinations. The most effective cytokines and cytokine combinations identified in vitro will be used by the Weiss group for in vivo testing. The B16 clones expressing the shortlisted cytokines or combinations will be used to generate melanoma BMs by intracardial injections. Cytokine expression will be activated with doxycycline and the tumor response will be monitored using MRI and survival measurement. Aim 3: Design of tumor-targeting immunocytokines. Potent cytokines that reactivate the immune compartment and eradicated melanoma BMs will be incorporated into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins (immunocytokines) targeting species-conserved tumor antigens highly expressed in melanoma BMs. Employing melanoma BMs murine models and our advanced technologies from previous objectives, we aim at breaking through the classical dichotomy of response/no-response and into cycles of model-driven optimization. This will ultimately pinpoint the most effective constructs for the IND stage. Impact: Our team of world leaders in immunology, brain tumors and computer science research is ideally suited to deliver this disruptive proposal.

Impact: Our team of world leaders in immunology, brain tumors and computer science research is ideally suited to deliver this disruptive proposal. Ido Amit, an authority in single-cell immuno-oncology, and Amos Tanay, a leading figure in algorithm development for single cell biology, have co-developed several innovative single-cell computational tools. Tobias Weiss, an early-stage physician-scientist specializing in brain malignancies, developed promising immunocytokines against glioblastoma, now in clinical trials. The team has recently published and demonstrated that immunocytokines induce significant anti-tumor responses in both mice and glioblastoma patients.
Title: Developing AI tailored immunocytokines to target melanoma brain metastases

Background: Brain metastases (BMs) occur in almost 50% of patients with metastatic melanoma, resulting in a dismal prognosis with a poor overall survival for most patients. Metastases arise from primary tumor clones capable of adapting to new environments and are frequently resistant to therapies to which the primary tumor was sensitive. Particularly in BMs, the milieu of the brain creates a highly immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). Single-cell technologies together with machine learning have emerged as powerful tools to decipher complex interactions between cells in the TME, enabling development of data driven designs of immunotherapies. In this project, we aim to harness these tools to gain in depth understanding of melanoma BM TMEs immunosuppression, and to develop novel cytokine-based approaches that turn the BM TME from immunologically “cold” to “hot”.

Aim 1: Developing BM TME models with holistic, multimodal AI-driven analysis. We will rely on orthotopic immunocompetent murine models for melanoma BMs developed by the Weiss group together with the large cohort of patient clinical samples collected by the Weiss group and our collaborators in hospitals in Israel. These will be used to create single-cell atlases of immune, stromal and tumor cells from human and murine BMs. We will go significantly beyond the state of the art, by building integrated tissue models from single cells and holistically inferring their physiology and potential drug response. We will use novel technology developed in our groups for cell temporal tracking (ZMAN-seq, differentiation flows), cell-cell interactions (PIC-seq), and improved strategies for AI analysis of spatial transcriptomics with Stereo-seq. Our tools will identify shared processes and pathways between our models and human patient TMEs, enabling us to compile a list of cytokines with potentially high antitumor activity in melanoma BMs.

Aim 2: Preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models. The cytokines identified in Aim 1 will be tested in vitro and in vivo. First, cytokine-secreting clones of brain tropic melanoma cell lines will be created by the Amit group by transducing them to conditionally (dox-induced) express a broad spectrum of promising BM immune re-activation candidate cytokines. These clones will be used to create tumor spheroids containing one or multiple cytokine cassettes and will be co-cultured with immune cells. By adding doxycycline, we will test varied cytokine combinations. The most effective cytokines and cytokine combinations identified in vitro will be used by the Weiss group for in vivo testing. The B16 clones expressing the shortlisted cytokines or combinations will be used to generate melanoma BMs by intracardial injections. Cytokine expression will be activated with doxycycline and the tumor response will be monitored using MRI and survival measurement.

Aim 3: Design of tumor-targeting immunocytokines. Potent cytokines that reactivate the immune compartment and eradicated melanoma BMs will be incorporated into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins (immunocytokines) targeting species-conserved tumor antigens highly expressed in melanoma BMs. Employing melanoma BMs murine models and our advanced technologies from previous objectives, we aim at breaking through the classical dichotomy of response/no-response and into cycles of model-driven optimization. This will ultimately pinpoint the most effective constructs for the IND stage.

Impact: Our team of world leaders in immunology, brain tumors and computer science research is ideally suited to deliver this disruptive proposal. Ido Amit, an authority in single-cell immuno-oncology, and Amos Tanay, a leading figure in algorithm development for single cell biology, have co-developed several innovative single-cell computational tools. Tobias Weiss, an early-stage physician-scientist specializing in brain malignancies, developed promising immunocytokines against glioblastoma, now in clinical trials. The team has recently published and demonstrated that immunocytokines induce significant anti-tumor responses in both mice and glioblastoma patients.
